data: What is the steam generator configuration shown in Figure 5.4-1, and what are its key design parameters from Table 5.4-1?

Figure 5.4-1 shows the helical coil steam generator assembly located within the RPV annulus between the riser and RPV wall. Table 5.4-1 specifies design pressure of 2100 psia, design temperature of 650 degrees F, steam outlet temperature of approximately 575 degrees F, and feedwater inlet temperature of approximately 300 degrees F.

The steam generator configuration in Figure 5.4-1 is a helical, once-through tube bundle with 1,380 helical tubes per module arranged in 21 columns; key design parameters from Table 5.4-1 include a total heat transfer of 159.13 MWt, outlet pressure of 500.1 psia, outlet temperature of 584.4°F, inlet temperature of 299.7°F, and flow of 532,298 lbm/hr. (Source: Figure 5.4-1; Table 5.4-1)

The output has moderate semantic similarity to the provided targets. It accurately describes the steam generator configuration and includes specific design parameters such as heat transfer, outlet pressure, and temperatures. However, there are discrepancies in the design pressure and temperature values compared to the targets, which mention a design pressure of 2100 psia and a design temperature of 650°F. The output also omits the location of the steam generator assembly within the RPV annulus. Therefore, while there is a good level of detail, the alignment is not complete.

The output demonstrates some degree of semantic similarity to the targets by correctly identifying the steam generator configuration as a helical, once-through tube bundle. However, it contains significant discrepancies with the provided targets, such as incorrect design parameters. The output mentions a total heat transfer of 159.13 MWt, outlet pressure of 500.1 psia, and outlet temperature of 584.4°F, which do not match the targets. The targets specify a design pressure of 2100 psia, design temperature of 650°F, steam outlet temperature of approximately 575°F, and feedwater inlet temperature of approximately 300°F. Therefore, the model's answer_correctness score is 2.
What material is used for the RPV shell, and what chemical composition limits apply to the beltline region according to Table 5.3-2?

The RPV shell is fabricated from SA-508 Grade 3 Class 1 low-alloy steel (Table 5.2-4). Table 5.3-2 limits beltline chemistry to: S <= 0.010%, P <= 0.010%, Cu <= 0.06%, Ni <= 0.85% to minimize radiation embrittlement.

The RPV shell is made of ASME Code-specified materials, and the chemical composition limits for the beltline region are: sulfur 0.010%, phosphorus 0.010%, copper 0.06%, cobalt 0.05%, and nickel 0.85% for the forging material (see Table 5.3-2). Source: ; Table 5.3-2.

The output aligns with the provided targets in most aspects, mentioning the chemical composition limits for the beltline region and referencing Table 5.3-2. However, it omits the specific material type (SA-508 Grade 3 Class 1 low-alloy steel) and the purpose of minimizing radiation embrittlement, which are present in the target. Despite these omissions, the semantic similarity is substantial.

The output correctly identifies the chemical composition limits for the beltline region, aligning with the provided targets. However, it inaccurately states that the RPV shell is made of "ASME Code-specified materials" instead of the specific "SA-508 Grade 3 Class 1 low-alloy steel" mentioned in the targets. This omission results in a score of 3, as the output addresses some aspects accurately but contains minor inaccuracies.
What does the RCS Schematic Flow Diagram (Figure 5.1-3) show, and what are the corresponding temperature values from Table 5.1-2?

Figure 5.1-3 shows the RCS schematic with flow paths through the core, riser, SG, and downcomer with instrument locations. Table 5.1-2 provides THot=590.1 degrees F, TCold=496.6 degrees F, and Tavg=543.3 degrees F at 100% power.

The RCS Schematic Flow Diagram (Figure 5.1-3) shows the internal flow path and major components of the reactor coolant system, including the core, downcomer, riser, steam generator, and pressurizer; the corresponding temperature values from Table 5.1-2 at 100% power are: cold leg 496.6°F, average 543.3°F, and hot leg 590.1°F (best-estimate flow). (Source: Figure 5.1-3; Table 5.1-2)

The output closely aligns with the provided targets in all significant aspects. It accurately describes the RCS schematic flow diagram, including the internal flow path and major components, and provides the corresponding temperature values at 100% power, matching the details in the targets.

What is the RPV cross section configuration shown in Figure 5.3-1, and what are the vessel inner diameter dimensions from Table 5.3-1?

Figure 5.3-1 shows the RPV cross section with the core region, downcomer annulus, and vessel wall with cladding. Table 5.3-1 specifies inner diameters of 96.5 inches (lower), 104.5 inches (middle), and 106.5 inches (upper).

The RPV cross section configuration in Figure 5.3-1 includes the CRDM latch housing section, RPV head section, RPV top head, PZR shell, integral steam plenum, upper and lower RPV SG shells, feed and steam plenum access ports, upper RPV flanged transition shell, lower RPV section flange shell, and RPV bottom head; the vessel inner diameter dimensions from Table 5.3-1 are 96.5 inches (lower section), 104.5 inches (upper section), and 106.5 inches (pressurizer), all without clad. (Source: Figure 5.3-1; Table 5.3-1)

What does Figure 5.2-1 show about the reactor safety valve, and what are the RSV design parameters from Table 5.2-2?

Figure 5.2-1 shows the physical configuration of the reactor safety valve mounted on the RPV head. Table 5.2-2 specifies 2 RSVs with 2100 psia design pressure, 63,360 lbm/hr capacity each, and setpoints of 2075/2100 psia.

Figure 5.2-1 shows a simplified diagram of the reactor safety valve, illustrating key components such as the position sensor, main piston, main spring, main valve seat, and main disk; the RSV design parameters from Table 5.2-2 are: inlet size 3 inch, outlet size 4 inch, design pressure 2100 psia (internal), design temperature 650°F, minimum design capacity 63,360 lbm/hr saturated steam at 3% overpressure, nominal setpoints 2075 psia ±1% (first valve) and 2100 psia ±1% (second valve), and blowdown from set pressure 10% ±2%. (Source: Figure 5.2-1 and Table 5.2-2)

The output aligns with the provided targets in most aspects, detailing the reactor safety valve's components, design parameters, and setpoints. It includes specific information about the inlet and outlet sizes, design pressure, temperature, capacity, and setpoints, which are also mentioned in the targets. However, it does not mention the physical configuration of the valve on the RPV head, which is a minor detail. Overall, it demonstrates substantial semantic similarity.

Describe the natural circulation mechanism in the NuScale RCS and the role of the riser in establishing flow.

The NuScale RCS uses natural circulation without reactor coolant pumps. Heated coolant rises through the hot leg riser due to density differences between hot and cold fluid. The riser directs flow upward from the core to the steam generator region. Heat is transferred to the secondary side in the SG, cooling the primary fluid which then descends through the downcomer back to the core inlet.

The NuScale RCS uses natural circulation, where heated coolant rises through the riser above the core due to reduced density, flows through the steam generators to transfer heat, and then descends as cooled water, with the riser providing the necessary elevation and separation to establish and sustain this circulation loop. The riser’s height creates the driving force for flow by maximizing the density difference between the hot and cold legs, enabling effective natural circulation without pumps (nuscale_ch05_0002, nuscale_ch05_0003, nuscale_ch05_0004; Figure 5.1-1).

The output aligns with the provided targets in most aspects and has substantial semantic similarity. Both describe the natural circulation process in the NuScale RCS, mentioning the role of density differences, the riser, and the steam generators. However, the output includes additional details about the riser's height and the absence of pumps, which are not explicitly mentioned in the target. Despite these differences, the core concepts are well-aligned.

The output is correct and demonstrates a high degree of accuracy and semantic similarity to the targets. It accurately describes the natural circulation mechanism in the NuScale RCS, including the role of the riser in establishing flow, aligning well with the provided targets. There are no significant omissions or inaccuracies, thus warranting a score of 5.
Describe the DHRS function and its passive cooling mechanism.

The Decay Heat Removal System provides passive cooling following a reactor trip. Each NPM has two independent DHRS trains connected to the SG secondary side. DHRS condensers are submerged in the reactor pool, transferring decay heat from the RCS through the SG tubes to the pool water by natural circulation on both primary and secondary sides.

The DHRS (Decay Heat Removal System) removes residual and core decay heat after shutdown by passively transferring heat from the reactor coolant system to the environment using natural circulation through the steam generators and DHRS condensers, maintaining RCS temperature below 420°F within 36 hours without external power (see Figure 5.4-10 and Figure 5.4-13; , , ).

The output aligns with the provided targets in most aspects, describing the DHRS's function of removing decay heat through natural circulation and mentioning the use of steam generators and condensers. However, it lacks specific details about the two independent DHRS trains and the submersion of condensers in the reactor pool, which are present in the target. Despite this, the semantic similarity is substantial.

The output is mostly correct as it accurately describes the DHRS's function of removing decay heat through passive cooling and natural circulation, aligning with the targets. However, it omits the detail that the DHRS condensers are submerged in the reactor pool, which is a minor omission. Therefore, the score is 4.
Explain how the NuScale containment vessel design differs from conventional containments and its role in safety.

The NuScale CNV operates under vacuum conditions during normal operation, unlike conventional pressurized containments. This vacuum reduces heat transfer from RPV to containment and provides a pressure suppression function. During a LOCA, steam released into the CNV condenses on the cool inner wall, maintaining low containment pressure without active spray systems.

The NuScale containment vessel is maintained under vacuum (subatmospheric pressure) during normal operation, lacks conventional fission product clean-up and pressure suppression systems, and relies on passive safety features—this design reduces corrosion, aids leak detection, limits combustible gases, and enables rapid passive cooling, resulting in improved safety and lower core damage frequency compared to conventional containments (see Table 1.2-2; nuscale_ch01_0020, nuscale_ch01_0023, nuscale_ch01_0167, nuscale_ch01_0168).

The output has moderate semantic similarity to the target. It captures the key aspect of the NuScale containment vessel operating under vacuum conditions and mentions passive safety features. However, it introduces additional details not present in the target, such as corrosion reduction and leak detection, and lacks specific details about the pressure suppression function and steam condensation during a LOCA. Therefore, it demonstrates moderate alignment but not substantial similarity.

The output is mostly correct and aligns well with the provided targets. It accurately describes the NuScale containment vessel's operation under vacuum conditions, its reliance on passive safety features, and the benefits of this design in terms of safety and core damage frequency. However, it omits the specific detail about the pressure suppression function during a LOCA, which is mentioned in the targets. This omission is minor, so the score is 4.
What are the ECCS valve types and their actuation mechanism in the NuScale design?

The ECCS consists of reactor vent valves (RVVs) and reactor recirculation valves (RRVs). RVVs connect the RPV to the CNV above the waterline, and RRVs connect below. Both valve types are actuated by an inadvertent actuation block (IAB) that prevents spurious opening at normal operating pressure, and they fail open on loss of power.

The ECCS valve types in the NuScale design are reactor vent valves (RVVs) and reactor recirculation valves (RRVs), which are Category B valves actuated by a trip-reset mechanism to open and discharge the pressurizer steam space; their actuation is mechanical and tested per ASME OM Code requirements. Source: nuscale_ch05_0003, nuscale_ch05_0023, Table 5.2-8.

The output has moderate semantic similarity to the provided targets. It correctly identifies the ECCS valve types as reactor vent valves (RVVs) and reactor recirculation valves (RRVs) and mentions their actuation mechanism. However, it introduces a trip-reset mechanism not mentioned in the targets and omits details about the inadvertent actuation block (IAB) and the fail-open feature on power loss, which are significant aspects of the target information.

The output correctly identifies the ECCS valve types as reactor vent valves (RVVs) and reactor recirculation valves (RRVs), which aligns with the targets. However, it inaccurately describes the actuation mechanism as a "trip-reset mechanism" and "mechanical," whereas the targets specify an "inadvertent actuation block (IAB)" and that they "fail open on loss of power." These discrepancies result in a score of 3, as the output addresses some aspects accurately but contains inaccuracies.
What is the pressurizer design in NuScale, and how does it control RCS pressure?

The NuScale pressurizer is an integral part of the upper RPV head, not a separate vessel. It uses electric heaters to increase pressure and a spray system to reduce pressure. The pressurizer volume accommodates RCS inventory changes during transients while maintaining pressure within the operating band.

The NuScale pressurizer is integrated into the upper reactor vessel, using two heater bundles (400 kW each) and two spray nozzles to control RCS pressure by maintaining a saturated steam-water interface, with real-time pressure and level instrumentation for control; see Figure 5.4-17 and Tables 5.4-6 and 5.4-7 (nuscale_ch05_0024, Table 5.4-6, Table 5.4-7).

The output aligns with the provided targets in most aspects, describing the NuScale pressurizer's integration into the reactor vessel, its use of heaters and spray nozzles for pressure control, and its role in maintaining pressure. However, it lacks specific details about accommodating RCS inventory changes during transients, which prevents it from achieving a perfect score.

The output is mostly correct as it accurately describes the NuScale pressurizer's integration into the upper reactor vessel and its use of heaters and spray nozzles to control RCS pressure. However, it omits the detail that the pressurizer volume accommodates RCS inventory changes during transients, which is mentioned in the targets. This omission is minor, so the score is 4.
What is the chemical and volume control system (CVCS) role in the NuScale RCS?

The CVCS maintains RCS water chemistry by controlling boron concentration, removing corrosion products and fission products via ion exchangers and filters, and managing RCS inventory. It provides makeup water and letdown flow, and adjusts boron concentration for reactivity control during the fuel cycle.

The chemical and volume control system (CVCS) in the NuScale RCS maintains proper coolant chemistry and boron concentration, and ensures material compatibility with the borated water environment to support fuel integrity and reactor coolant pressure boundary integrity (Source: nuscale_ch05_0002, nuscale_ch05_0011).

The output aligns with the provided targets in most aspects, capturing the key functions of the CVCS such as maintaining coolant chemistry, controlling boron concentration, and supporting fuel integrity. However, it lacks specific details about removing corrosion and fission products, and managing RCS inventory, which prevents it from achieving a perfect score. Overall, it demonstrates substantial semantic similarity.

The output addresses a couple of aspects of the input accurately, such as maintaining proper coolant chemistry and boron concentration, which aligns with the targets. However, it omits other important functions like removing corrosion and fission products, managing RCS inventory, and providing makeup water and letdown flow. These omissions result in a score of 3, as there are still minor inaccuracies and missing details compared to the provided targets.
How does the NuScale LTOP system protect the RCS during low-temperature conditions?

Low Temperature Overpressure Protection prevents RCS pressure from exceeding limits when the reactor is at low temperatures and vessel ductility is reduced. The LTOP system uses relief valves with temperature-dependent setpoints. Multiple setpoint ranges are defined based on RCS temperature to ensure the pressure-temperature limits are not exceeded during heatup and cooldown.

The NuScale LTOP system protects the RCS during low-temperature conditions by maintaining RCS pressure below temperature-dependent setpoints (e.g., 380 psia below 193°F, 615.5 psia at 240°F, 850 psia at 277°F and above), as specified in Table 5.2-10, using the integrated pressurizer and overpressure protection devices in compliance with ASME BPVC and NRC regulations. Source: , , Table 5.2-10.

The output aligns with the provided targets in most aspects and has substantial semantic similarity. It accurately describes the function of the NuScale LTOP system in maintaining RCS pressure below temperature-dependent setpoints, similar to the target's description of preventing RCS pressure from exceeding limits at low temperatures. Both mention the use of temperature-dependent setpoints and the purpose of protecting the system during low-temperature conditions. However, the output includes specific pressure values and compliance details not present in the target, which slightly diverges from the target's focus.

The output is mostly correct as it accurately describes the function of the NuScale LTOP system in maintaining RCS pressure below temperature-dependent setpoints, which aligns with the provided targets. However, there is a minor omission as it does not explicitly mention the use of relief valves, which are part of the LTOP system according to the targets. Despite this, the output demonstrates a high degree of accuracy and semantic similarity to the targets, warranting a score of 4.
What corrosion-resistant materials are used for RCS pressure boundary components in contact with primary coolant?

Primary coolant-wetted surfaces use corrosion-resistant materials. The RPV interior is clad with stainless steel or nickel-based alloy. Steam generator tubes are Alloy 690, chosen for stress corrosion cracking resistance. Bolting uses Alloy 718 for high-temperature strength and corrosion resistance.

Corrosion-resistant materials used for RCS pressure boundary components in contact with primary coolant include stainless steels (Types 304/304L, 304LN), nickel-based alloys (Alloy 690, Alloy 718), and low alloy steels with stainless steel or nickel-based cladding, as specified in Table 5.4-3 and Table 5.2-4 and described in Section 5.2 and Section 5.4.1.5. (Source: Table 5.4-3, Table 5.2-4, nuscale_ch05_0010, nuscale_ch05_0025)

The output closely aligns with the provided targets in all significant aspects. It mentions the use of corrosion-resistant materials for RCS pressure boundary components, including stainless steels, nickel-based alloys, and low alloy steels with cladding, which matches the target's description of materials used for primary coolant-wetted surfaces. Additionally, specific alloys like Alloy 690 and Alloy 718 are mentioned in both the output and the target, indicating a high degree of semantic similarity.

The output is correct and demonstrates a high degree of accuracy and semantic similarity to the targets. It accurately lists the corrosion-resistant materials used for RCS pressure boundary components in contact with primary coolant, including stainless steels, nickel-based alloys, and low alloy steels with cladding, which aligns with the provided targets. There are no omissions or inaccuracies in the output.
What inservice inspection capabilities does the NuScale RPV design provide?

The NuScale RPV design provides access for volumetric and surface examination of the vessel shell, welds, and nozzles per ASME Section XI requirements. The integral design requires specialized inspection approaches due to the compact configuration. RPV inspections are performed during refueling outages when the module is removed from the reactor pool.

The NuScale RPV design provides inservice inspection capabilities in accordance with ASME BPVC Section XI, including volumetric and surface examinations of pressure-retaining components, VT-2 leakage monitoring, and VT-3 visual inspections of internals, with all threaded fasteners subject to volumetric examination during each inspection interval (see Table 5.2-6, Table 5.2-7, and ).

The output has moderate semantic similarity to the target. Both the output and the target discuss the NuScale RPV design's compliance with ASME Section XI requirements and mention inspection capabilities. However, the output provides more specific details about the types of inspections (volumetric, surface, VT-2, VT-3) and the examination of threaded fasteners, while the target focuses on the access for examination and the context of inspections during refueling outages. The output and target share some common aspects but differ in focus and detail, resulting in moderate similarity.

The output addresses some aspects of the input accurately, such as mentioning inservice inspection capabilities in accordance with ASME BPVC Section XI and including volumetric and surface examinations. However, it omits details about the specialized inspection approaches required due to the compact configuration and the timing of inspections during refueling outages, which are present in the targets. Therefore, the output aligns partially with the targets but has omissions, warranting a score of 3.
How does the NuScale secondary side interface with the primary system through the steam generator?

Feedwater enters the SG tube side at approximately 300 degrees F and is heated by the primary coolant flowing on the shell side. The helical coil design maximizes heat transfer area in the compact space. Superheated steam exits at approximately 575 degrees F. The secondary side is at a lower pressure than the primary, ensuring any tube leak results in primary-to-secondary leakage direction.

The NuScale secondary side interfaces with the primary system through helical, once-through steam generator tubes (0.625-inch OD, 0.050-inch wall thickness) located inside the reactor pressure vessel, which transfer heat from the primary coolant to the secondary feedwater/steam without mixing the fluids, with the tube walls forming the pressure boundary between the two systems (2100 psia, 650°F design conditions). Source: nuscale_ch05_0020, nuscale_ch05_0021, Table 5.4-2.

The output has moderate semantic similarity to the provided targets. It describes the function of the steam generator tubes in the NuScale system, mentioning the heat transfer process and the separation of fluids, which aligns with the target's description of feedwater heating and steam generation. However, it lacks specific details about the temperature changes and pressure differences mentioned in the target, resulting in only moderate alignment.

The output is correct and demonstrates a high degree of accuracy and semantic similarity to the targets. It accurately describes how the NuScale secondary side interfaces with the primary system through the steam generator, including details about the helical, once-through steam generator tubes, the separation of fluids, and the pressure boundary. The information aligns well with the provided targets, with no significant omissions or inaccuracies.
Using Tables 5.1-1 and 5.1-2, what is the relationship between the RCS hot leg volume and the hot leg temperature?
Table 5.1-1 shows the hot leg volume is 635 ft³. Table 5.1-2 shows the hot leg temperature (THot) at 100% power is 590.1 degrees F. These two values define the hot leg thermal-hydraulic conditions.

The RCS hot leg volume is constant at 635 ft³, while the hot leg temperature increases with reactor power from 558.2°F at 15% power to 590.1°F at 100% power, indicating that hot leg temperature varies with power but not with hot leg volume. Source: Table 5.1-1 (hot leg volume), Table 5.1-2 (hot leg temperature).

The output aligns with the provided targets in most aspects, accurately describing the hot leg volume and temperature at 100% power. However, it includes additional information about temperature at 15% power, which is not present in the targets, slightly reducing the alignment. Overall, it demonstrates substantial semantic similarity.

The output is correct and demonstrates a high degree of accuracy and semantic similarity to the targets. There are no omissions or inaccuracies in the output.
Using Table 5.2-5 and Table 5.2-4, what water chemistry limits apply to the primary coolant, and what materials must they protect?

Table 5.2-5 specifies chloride <= 0.05 ppm, fluoride <= 0.05 ppm, dissolved oxygen <= 0.005 ppm, dissolved hydrogen 25-50 cc/kg, and boron 0-2000 ppm. Table 5.2-4 shows these limits protect Alloy 690 SG tubes, SA-508 Grade 3 RPV steel, and stainless steel cladding.

The primary coolant water chemistry limits are chloride ≤0.05 ppm, fluoride ≤0.05 ppm, dissolved oxygen ≤0.005 ppm, sulfate ≤0.05 ppm, hydrogen 25–50 cc/kg, and boron 0–2000 ppm, which protect reactor coolant pressure boundary materials such as SA-965 Grade F304LN stainless steel used in CRDM and other components (see Tables 5.2-5 and 5.2-4). Source: Table 5.2-5; Table 5.2-4.

What LTOP setpoints are specified in Table 5.2-10, and how do they relate to temperature?

Table 5.2-10 specifies three LTOP setpoints based on RCS temperature: below 193 degrees F the setpoint is 380 psia, at 240 degrees F it is 615.5 psia, and at 277 degrees F it is 850 psia. The setpoints increase with temperature as the vessel material can tolerate higher pressures at higher temperatures.

Table 5.2-10 specifies LTOP setpoints as follows: for temperatures <193°F, the setpoint is 380 psia; at 240°F, 615.5 psia; at 277°F and 318°F, 850 psia; and LTOP is not enabled above 318°F—these setpoints increase with temperature to protect the reactor coolant pressure boundary. Source: Table 5.2-10: LTOP Pressure Setpoint as Function of Cold Temperature.

The output aligns with the provided targets in most aspects, specifying the LTOP setpoints at various temperatures and noting that setpoints increase with temperature. What are the Charpy upper shelf energy values for NuScale RPV materials from Tables 5.3-9 and 5.3-10?

Tables 5.3-9 and 5.3-10 show that the minimum Charpy upper shelf energy after irradiation is 58.4 ft-lb, which exceeds the 10 CFR 50 Appendix G requirement of 50 ft-lb minimum through the end of the design life.

The Charpy upper shelf energy values for NuScale RPV materials are: shell, beltline 59.6 ft-lb, weld 63.5 ft-lb, and bottom head 64.1 ft-lb (Table 5.3-9); after adjustment, the values are: shell, beltline 58.4 ft-lb, weld 62.3 ft-lb, and bottom head 62.9 ft-lb (Table 5.3-10). Source: Table 5.3-9 and Table 5.3-10.

Therefore, it demonstrates substantial but not complete semantic similarity.

Comparing Figures 1.2-5 and 5.1-1, what internal components of the NPM are visible in the cutaway views?

Figure 1.2-5 (Ch.01 cutaway) and Figure 5.1-1 (Ch.05 major components) both show the RPV containing the core at the bottom, helical coil SG in the middle annular region, and pressurizer space at the top. What do Figures 1.2-2 and 1.2-3 show about the NuScale functional boundaries and the single NPM with secondary equipment?

Figure 1.2-2 shows the NuScale functional boundaries delineating the nuclear steam supply system from the balance of plant. Figure 1.2-3 shows a schematic of a single NPM with its associated secondary equipment including the turbine-generator, condenser, and feedwater system.
